name: NASA Missions
description: From Mercury's first astronauts to the James Webb Space Telescope — master the missions, programs, and pioneers that defined space exploration. Learn about Apollo Moon landings, Mars rovers, Voyager's interstellar journey, and the astronauts who made history.

facts.quizQuestion: What was the name of NASA's first crewed spaceflight program, which ran from 1958 to 1963?
facts.explanation: Project Mercury was NASA's first human spaceflight program, featuring single-astronaut spacecraft. It successfully placed five Americans in space and one in orbit before transitioning to the two-person Gemini program. Source: NASA.gov
facts.statement: Mercury was NASA's first crewed spaceflight program that ran from 1958 to 1963.
facts.wowFactor: Mercury capsules were so small that astronauts couldn't walk in them — they were shot into space like bullets and had to squeeze into a space barely larger than a telephone booth.
facts.visualDescription: Sleek silver capsule with a heat shield base, topped with a small conical escape tower, sitting on a Redstone rocket at the launch pad

variants.question: Which program was NASA's first crewed spaceflight initiative?
What was the name of the NASA program that preceded Gemini and taught basic spaceflight operations?
True or false: Mercury was NASA's first crewed spaceflight program.
NASA's first crewed spaceflight program was called ___ and ran from 1958 to 1963.

facts.quizQuestion: What was the name of Alan Shepard's suborbital spaceflight on May 5, 1961, making him the first American in space?
facts.explanation: Freedom 7 (Mercury-Redstone 3) carried astronaut Alan Shepard on a 15-minute suborbital flight, reaching an altitude of 116 km. Though he didn't reach orbit, Shepard became the first American in space, nine months after Soviet Yuri Gagarin. Source: NASA.gov
facts.statement: Freedom 7 was Alan Shepard's suborbital spaceflight on May 5, 1961, making him the first American in space.
facts.wowFactor: Alan Shepard had to urinate in his suit during the launch delay — there was no bathroom in the Mercury capsule and NASA didn't plan for such human necessities.
facts.visualDescription: Alan Shepard in a silver spacesuit inside the Freedom 7 capsule, with the Redstone rocket launching into a blue sky

variants.question: Which mission made Alan Shepard the first American in space?
On May 5, 1961, which NASA mission achieved the distinction of first American spaceflight?
True or false: Freedom 7 was Alan Shepard's orbital spaceflight.
Alan Shepard piloted ___ on May 5, 1961, becoming the first American in space with a suborbital flight.

facts.quizQuestion: What was the name of John Glenn's spaceflight on February 20, 1962, making him the first American to orbit Earth?
facts.explanation: Friendship 7 (Mercury-Atlas 6) carried astronaut John Glenn on the first American orbital flight, completing three orbits of Earth in just under five hours. Glenn became an American hero and later served as a U.S. Senator. Source: NASA.gov
facts.statement: Friendship 7 was John Glenn's spaceflight on February 20, 1962, making him the first American to orbit Earth.
facts.wowFactor: During Friendship 7's reentry, ground control worried a loose heat shield might burn up the capsule, but they didn't tell Glenn until after he landed safely — the shield was actually fine.
facts.visualDescription: Mercury capsule Friendship 7 orbiting Earth with the atmosphere visible below and stars in the background

variants.question: Which mission made John Glenn the first American to orbit Earth?
On February 20, 1962, which NASA mission achieved the first American orbital spaceflight?
True or false: John Glenn was the first American to orbit Earth aboard Friendship 7.
John Glenn piloted ___ on February 20, 1962, becoming the first American to orbit Earth.

facts.quizQuestion: What was the name of NASA's two-person crewed spaceflight program (1961-1966) that tested spacewalks and docking techniques?
facts.explanation: Project Gemini was the bridge between Mercury and Apollo, using two-person spacecraft to develop critical techniques like spacewalking (EVA), orbital rendezvous, and docking that were essential for the Apollo Moon missions. Source: NASA.gov
facts.statement: Gemini was NASA's two-person crewed spaceflight program that tested spacewalks and docking techniques.
facts.wowFactor: Gemini astronauts performed the first American spacewalk, the first crewed docking in space, and perfected techniques that would later land humans on the Moon.
facts.visualDescription: Two-person Gemini spacecraft with its distinctive shape docking with an Agena target vehicle in orbit

variants.question: Which NASA program bridged Mercury and Apollo with two-person spacecraft and practiced spacewalks?
What was the name of the NASA program where astronauts first tested spacewalks and orbital docking?
True or false: Gemini was a single-person spaceflight program.
NASA's ___ program (1961-1966) was the bridge between Mercury and Apollo, featuring two-person spacecraft.

facts.quizQuestion: Which NASA mission featured Ed White's first American spacewalk on June 3, 1965?
facts.explanation: Gemini 4 carried astronauts Gus Grissom and Ed White into orbit. During the mission, White performed America's first spacewalk (EVA), spending 21 minutes outside the capsule tethered to the spacecraft. This was a critical test for upcoming Moon missions. Source: NASA.gov
facts.statement: Gemini 4 featured Ed White's first American spacewalk on June 3, 1965.
facts.wowFactor: Ed White's spacewalk was so successful he didn't want to come back inside — he reportedly said 'It's the saddest moment of my life' when told to return to the capsule.
facts.visualDescription: Ed White in a white spacesuit floating outside Gemini 4, performing the first American spacewalk with Earth visible below

variants.question: Which mission is famous for Ed White's first American spacewalk?
On June 3, 1965, which NASA mission achieved the first American spacewalk?
True or false: Ed White performed the first American spacewalk on Gemini 4.
___ was the mission where Ed White performed America's first spacewalk on June 3, 1965.

facts.quizQuestion: Which NASA mission, commanded by Neil Armstrong on March 16, 1966, achieved the first crewed orbital docking in history?
facts.explanation: Gemini 8 accomplished the first crewed orbital docking when it rendezvoused and docked with an Agena target vehicle. During the mission, the docked vehicles began spinning dangerously, but Armstrong successfully undocked and regained control, preventing a catastrophic failure. This was crucial practice for Apollo. Source: NASA.gov
facts.statement: Gemini 8 achieved the first crewed orbital docking on March 16, 1966, commanded by Neil Armstrong.
facts.wowFactor: After docking, Gemini 8 began spinning uncontrollably — Armstrong had to undock and manually fire thrusters to stabilize the spacecraft, demonstrating the quick thinking that would later save Apollo 13.
facts.visualDescription: Gemini 8 spacecraft docked with the Agena target vehicle in orbit, with solar panels extended

variants.question: Which mission achieved the first crewed orbital docking in history?
Which NASA mission, commanded by Neil Armstrong, performed the first crewed docking in space?
True or false: Gemini 8 achieved the first crewed orbital docking.
___ was the mission that achieved the first crewed orbital docking on March 16, 1966, commanded by Neil Armstrong.

facts.quizQuestion: What was the name of NASA's program that successfully landed humans on the Moon between 1969 and 1972?
facts.explanation: Project Apollo was NASA's ambitious lunar exploration program that achieved President Kennedy's goal of landing humans on the Moon before 1970. Twelve astronauts walked on the Moon across six successful landing missions (Apollo 11, 12, 14, 15, 16, 17). Source: NASA.gov
facts.statement: Apollo was NASA's program that successfully landed humans on the Moon between 1969 and 1972.
facts.wowFactor: The Apollo program cost $152 billion (2023 dollars) and employed 400,000 people at its peak — it remains humanity's greatest exploration achievement.
facts.visualDescription: Lunar module Eagle on the Moon's surface with an astronaut in a white spacesuit standing nearby, with the lunar landscape and black sky

variants.question: Which NASA program achieved the Moon landings?
What was the name of NASA's program that fulfilled Kennedy's goal of landing humans on the Moon?
True or false: Apollo was NASA's lunar landing program.
___ was NASA's program that successfully landed 12 humans on the Moon between 1969 and 1972.

facts.quizQuestion: Which NASA mission ended in a tragic cabin fire on January 27, 1967, killing astronauts Gus Grissom, Ed White, and Roger Chaffee?
facts.explanation: Apollo 1 (AS-204) suffered a catastrophic cabin fire during a launch rehearsal test at Cape Kennedy. The pure oxygen atmosphere and flammable materials accelerated the fire's spread. The tragedy led to major safety redesigns, but the program resumed and achieved its Moon landing goals. Source: NASA.gov
facts.statement: Apollo 1 experienced a cabin fire on January 27, 1967, killing astronauts Gus Grissom, Ed White, and Roger Chaffee.
facts.wowFactor: The Apollo 1 fire was a turning point that fundamentally changed spacecraft design — investigations revealed the cabin had been pressurized with pure oxygen, a major fire hazard that was never used again.
facts.visualDescription: Apollo command module mounted on a test stand with disaster scene aftermath

variants.question: Which mission tragically ended in a cabin fire killing Grissom, White, and Chaffee?
Which NASA mission ended in tragedy on January 27, 1967, during a launch rehearsal?
True or false: Apollo 1 experienced a cabin fire during a launch rehearsal.
___ ended in a catastrophic cabin fire on January 27, 1967, killing three astronauts during launch rehearsal.

facts.quizQuestion: Which NASA mission first carried humans to orbit the Moon in December 1968 and captured the famous 'Earthrise' photograph?
facts.explanation: Apollo 8 was the first crewed spacecraft to reach the Moon, orbiting it 10 times in December 1968. Astronauts Frank Borman, Jim Lovell, and Bill Anders saw Earth rise above the lunar horizon and photographed it — one of the most iconic images ever taken. The mission proved navigation to the Moon was possible. Source: NASA.gov
facts.statement: Apollo 8 first carried humans to orbit the Moon in December 1968 and captured the famous 'Earthrise' photograph.
facts.wowFactor: The Earthrise photograph taken by Apollo 8 profoundly changed how humanity viewed itself — seeing our home as a fragile blue marble in the cosmic void inspired the environmental movement.
facts.visualDescription: The iconic Earthrise photograph showing Earth rising above the gray Moon's horizon against the black void of space

variants.question: Which mission first brought humans to orbit the Moon?
Which NASA mission captured the famous 'Earthrise' photograph in December 1968?
True or false: Apollo 8 was the first crewed mission to orbit the Moon.
___ was the first crewed mission to orbit the Moon in December 1968 and photographed the famous Earthrise image.

facts.quizQuestion: Which NASA mission carried the first humans to walk on the Moon in July 1969?
facts.explanation: Apollo 11 landed astronauts Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin on the Moon on July 20, 1969, while Michael Collins orbited above in the Command Module. Armstrong became the first human to set foot on the Moon, famously saying 'That's one small step for man, one giant leap for mankind.' Source: NASA.gov
facts.statement: Apollo 11 carried the first humans to walk on the Moon in July 1969.
facts.wowFactor: The Apollo 11 guidance computer had just 74 KB of memory — less than a modern calculator — yet it navigated humans 240,000 miles to the Moon and back.
facts.visualDescription: Lunar module Eagle descending toward the gray cratered Moon surface with Earth visible in the black sky behind

variants.question: Apollo 11 is most famous for which achievement?
Which mission achieved Kennedy's goal of landing a man on the Moon before the end of the 1960s?
True or false: Apollo 11 was the first mission to land humans on the Moon.
The first NASA mission to land humans on the Moon was ___ in July 1969.

facts.quizQuestion: Which NASA mission experienced an oxygen tank explosion in April 1970, forcing astronauts to use the Lunar Module as a lifeboat?
facts.explanation: Apollo 13, launched on April 11, 1970, suffered a catastrophic oxygen tank explosion two days into the mission. The three-person crew (Jim Lovell, Jack Swigert, Fred Haise) used the Lunar Module's life support systems to survive the journey back to Earth. Mission Control's quick thinking and the crew's resolve turned a potential tragedy into a successful rescue. Source: NASA.gov
facts.statement: Apollo 13 experienced an oxygen tank explosion in April 1970, requiring astronauts to use the Lunar Module as a lifeboat.
facts.wowFactor: When the oxygen tank exploded, astronaut Jack Swigert reported: 'Houston, we've had a problem' — the phrase became iconic because the crew and Mission Control calmly worked together to return safely against all odds.
facts.visualDescription: Apollo 13 command module with damaged oxygen tank, astronauts in the cramped lunar module during their journey back to Earth

variants.question: Which mission's crew had to use the Lunar Module as a lifeboat after an explosion?
Which NASA mission experienced the famous 'Houston, we've had a problem' moment in April 1970?
True or false: Apollo 13 was aborted due to an oxygen tank explosion.
___ experienced an oxygen tank explosion in April 1970 that forced the crew to use the Lunar Module to survive.

facts.quizQuestion: Which was the last crewed mission to land on the Moon in December 1972, with Gene Cernan being the final person to walk on the lunar surface?
facts.explanation: Apollo 17 was the final crewed lunar landing mission, carrying astronauts Eugene Cernan, Harrison Schmitt, and Ronald Evans. Gene Cernan was the last human to leave the Moon, making it the end of the Apollo era. The mission conducted the most ambitious scientific exploration of the Moon to date. Source: NASA.gov
facts.statement: Apollo 17 was the last crewed mission to land on the Moon in December 1972, with Gene Cernan as the final moonwalker.
facts.wowFactor: Gene Cernan, the last person to leave the Moon, left a photo of his daughter on the lunar surface as a goodbye. More than 50 years later, no human has returned to the Moon.
facts.visualDescription: Gene Cernan in a white spacesuit on the Moon with the Lunar Roving Vehicle in the background and mountains visible on the horizon

variants.question: Which mission was the last to land humans on the Moon?
Which NASA mission, in December 1972, ended human exploration of the Moon with Gene Cernan as the final moonwalker?
True or false: Apollo 17 was the last crewed lunar landing mission.
___ was the last crewed mission to land on the Moon in December 1972, with Gene Cernan as the final person to walk on the lunar surface.

facts.quizQuestion: Which NASA mission first used a Lunar Roving Vehicle (electric moon buggy) to explore the lunar surface?
facts.explanation: Apollo 15 (July-August 1971) was the first mission to use the Lunar Roving Vehicle, an electric-powered rover that allowed astronauts David Scott and James Irwin to explore far more of the Moon than would have been possible on foot. The LRV proved so successful it was used on subsequent missions and covered over 50 miles total across three Apollo flights. Source: NASA.gov
facts.statement: Apollo 15 was the first mission to use the Lunar Roving Vehicle to explore the Moon.
facts.wowFactor: The Lunar Roving Vehicle was designed to handle the harsh lunar environment and reached a top speed of 11 mph — it still sits on the Moon today, preserved in the vacuum.
facts.visualDescription: Astronaut driving the Lunar Roving Vehicle across the gray lunar landscape with the Lunar Module visible in the distance

variants.question: Which mission introduced the Lunar Roving Vehicle to explore the Moon?
Which NASA Moon mission first used an electric-powered rover called the Lunar Roving Vehicle?
True or false: The Lunar Roving Vehicle first appeared on Apollo 15.
___ was the first mission to use the Lunar Roving Vehicle, an electric moon buggy for lunar exploration.

facts.quizQuestion: How many humans have walked on the Moon throughout all of NASA's Apollo missions?
facts.explanation: Twelve astronauts have walked on the Moon across six successful Apollo landing missions: Apollo 11, 12, 14, 15, 16, and 17. Two astronauts walked on the Moon during each mission (except as mission commander and LM pilot), covering six different landing sites across the lunar surface. Source: NASA.gov
facts.statement: Twelve humans have walked on the Moon throughout all of NASA's Apollo missions.
facts.wowFactor: Those twelve moonwalkers represent less than 0.0000002% of all humans who have ever lived, making them part of one of humanity's rarest achievements.
facts.visualDescription: Composite image of six different lunar landing sites with astronauts in white spacesuits exploring different regions of the Moon

variants.question: How many people have ever walked on the Moon?
Across all Apollo lunar missions, what is the total number of astronauts who have walked on the Moon?
True or false: Twenty humans have walked on the Moon.
___ astronauts have walked on the Moon across the Apollo missions.

facts.quizQuestion: What was the name of NASA's reusable spacecraft system that conducted 135 orbital missions from 1981 to 2011?
facts.explanation: The Space Shuttle Program was a revolutionary system of reusable spacecraft designed to reduce launch costs and enable regular access to orbit. Five orbital shuttles flew 135 missions: Columbia, Challenger, Discovery, Atlantis, and Endeavour. Despite two tragic losses, the shuttle system deployed satellites, conducted scientific research, and built the International Space Station. Source: NASA.gov
facts.statement: The Space Shuttle was NASA's reusable spacecraft system that conducted 135 orbital missions from 1981 to 2011.
facts.wowFactor: The Space Shuttle was the first reusable spacecraft — it could be launched, landed, refurbished, and launched again, though this proved more expensive than initially expected.
facts.visualDescription: Space Shuttle Discovery attached to its orange external tank and white solid rocket boosters on the launch pad at sunrise

variants.question: Which NASA program consisted of reusable spacecraft like Columbia and Discovery?
What was the name of NASA's reusable spacecraft program that ran from 1981 to 2011?
True or false: The Space Shuttle was a reusable spacecraft system.
NASA's ___ program conducted 135 orbital missions using reusable spacecraft from 1981 to 2011.

facts.quizQuestion: Which NASA Space Shuttle mission, launched on April 12, 1981, was the first orbital test flight of the reusable spacecraft?
facts.explanation: STS-1 (Space Transportation System 1) carried astronauts John Young and Robert Crippen on a 54.5-hour orbital mission aboard Space Shuttle Columbia. It was the first crewed orbital flight of a reusable spacecraft and proved the shuttle concept could work. The mission was a critical milestone in spaceflight technology. Source: NASA.gov
facts.statement: STS-1 was the first orbital Space Shuttle mission, launched on April 12, 1981, aboard Columbia.
facts.wowFactor: STS-1 launched exactly 20 years after Yuri Gagarin became the first human in space — the Space Shuttle's first test flight happened on the same day the space age turned two decades old.
facts.visualDescription: Space Shuttle Columbia lifting off from Kennedy Space Center on a column of flame with full external tank and solid rocket boosters attached

variants.question: Which Space Shuttle mission was the first orbital test flight in 1981?
Which NASA mission launched the first Space Shuttle on April 12, 1981?
True or false: STS-1 was the first crewed orbital flight of the Space Shuttle.
___ was the first Space Shuttle orbital mission, launched on April 12, 1981, aboard Columbia.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Space Shuttle broke apart 73 seconds after launch on January 28, 1986, killing all seven astronauts aboard due to an O-ring failure?
facts.explanation: Space Shuttle Challenger disintegrated during ascent due to failure of an O-ring in the right solid rocket booster, which allowed hot gases to escape and weaken the structural attachment. The tragedy killed astronauts Dick Scobee, Michael Smith, Judith Resnik, Ellison Onizuka, Ronald McNair, Gregory Jarvis, and teacher-in-space Christa McAuliffe. The accident led to major redesigns in shuttle safety. Source: NASA.gov
facts.statement: Challenger broke apart during launch on January 28, 1986, killing all seven astronauts aboard.
facts.wowFactor: The Challenger disaster was watched live by millions, including students in classrooms across America who were watching their teacher-in-space Christa McAuliffe launch. It fundamentally changed how NASA approached risk.
facts.visualDescription: Space Shuttle Challenger on the launch pad on a cold morning, with ice visible on the external tank structure

variants.question: Which Space Shuttle broke apart during launch in 1986?
Which Space Shuttle disaster killed seven astronauts on January 28, 1986?
True or false: Challenger broke apart due to an O-ring failure.
___ broke apart 73 seconds after launch on January 28, 1986, in a disaster caused by O-ring failure.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Space Shuttle broke apart during reentry on February 1, 2003, killing all seven astronauts due to damage from a foam strike?
facts.explanation: Space Shuttle Columbia disintegrated during reentry after foam insulation from the external tank struck the left wing during launch, creating a hole that went undetected. Hot gases entered the wing structure during reentry, causing structural failure. The seven-person crew, including Israeli astronaut Ilan Ramon, perished. The disaster led to another major redesign of shuttle safety protocols. Source: NASA.gov
facts.statement: Columbia broke apart during reentry on February 1, 2003, killing all seven astronauts aboard.
facts.wowFactor: The Columbia disaster investigation took two years and led to the discovery that NASA had silenced engineers who had warned about the foam strike danger — a stark reminder that organizational culture matters more than raw technology.
facts.visualDescription: Space Shuttle Columbia reentering Earth's atmosphere, with heat tiles and structural damage visible on the left wing

variants.question: Which Space Shuttle broke apart during reentry in 2003?
Which Space Shuttle disaster occurred on February 1, 2003, due to foam insulation damage?
True or false: Columbia broke apart due to a foam strike on the wing.
___ broke apart during reentry on February 1, 2003, after foam damage to its left wing.

facts.quizQuestion: Which Space Shuttle mission in December 1993 repaired the Hubble Space Telescope's flawed mirror with corrective optics?
facts.explanation: STS-61 aboard Space Shuttle Atlantis carried astronauts who performed the first Hubble Space Telescope servicing mission. Astronauts installed corrective optics (COSTAR) to fix the spherical aberration in Hubble's primary mirror that had blurred images. This repair mission transformed Hubble from a scientific embarrassment into one of the most productive observatories ever. Source: NASA.gov
facts.statement: STS-61 was the Space Shuttle mission that repaired Hubble's flawed mirror with corrective optics in December 1993.
facts.wowFactor: The Hubble repair mission saved one of NASA's most expensive and famous instruments from becoming a complete failure — it cost $400 million and was initially called 'the most expensive mistake in space history.'
facts.visualDescription: Astronaut performing an EVA in the Hubble Space Telescope's cargo bay, working on the optical corrective system

variants.question: Which Space Shuttle mission repaired the Hubble Space Telescope?
Which Space Shuttle mission in December 1993 fixed Hubble's blurry vision with corrective optics?
True or false: STS-61 was the first Hubble servicing mission.
___ was the Space Shuttle mission that successfully repaired the Hubble Space Telescope in December 1993.

facts.quizQuestion: What was the name of NASA's first space station, which operated from 1973 to 1979?
facts.explanation: Skylab was NASA's first space station, successfully orbited in 1973 and inhabited by three crews totaling 171 days of occupation. It conducted groundbreaking experiments in astronomy, Earth observation, and materials science. The station was damaged during launch but was repaired by the first crew. Skylab reentered Earth's atmosphere on July 11, 1979. Source: NASA.gov
facts.statement: Skylab was NASA's first space station, which operated from 1973 to 1979.
facts.wowFactor: Skylab was built from the third stage of a Saturn V rocket — astronauts had to repair it in orbit after a collision damaged its thermal protection, making it the first on-orbit space station repair.
facts.visualDescription: Skylab space station in orbit with its distinctive cylindrical shape and large solar arrays extended against the darkness of space

variants.question: Which was NASA's first space station?
What was the name of the first American space station that operated in the 1970s?
True or false: Skylab was NASA's first space station.
___ was NASA's first space station, operated from 1973 to 1979.

facts.quizQuestion: Which space station reentered Earth's atmosphere over Australia on July 11, 1979, leading Australia to jokingly fine NASA for littering?
facts.explanation: Skylab, NASA's first space station, came out of orbit on July 11, 1979, and broke apart over the Indian Ocean and Australia. Debris scattered across Western Australia. In response, the Australian government humorously sent NASA a mock bill for $400 for littering, creating one of space history's most amusing moments. Source: NASA.gov
facts.statement: Skylab reentered Earth's atmosphere over Australia on July 11, 1979.
facts.wowFactor: When Skylab fell to Earth, a small town in Western Australia humorously charged NASA an A$400 fine for littering. NASA never paid, but the town now celebrates the day every July 11 as 'Skylab Day.'
facts.visualDescription: Skylab breaking apart during reentry with fiery trails across the Australian sky

variants.question: Which space station reentered Earth's atmosphere over Australia?
Which NASA space station fell to Earth over Australia in 1979, causing a humorous international incident?
True or false: Skylab reentered the atmosphere over Australia and Australia jokingly fined NASA for littering.
___ reentered Earth's atmosphere over Australia on July 11, 1979, leading to a humorous fine for littering.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the name of the orbiting laboratory where astronauts from multiple nations have lived continuously since November 2, 2000?
facts.explanation: The International Space Station (ISS) is the largest structure ever built in space, orbiting Earth at 17,500 mph. It has been continuously inhabited since November 2, 2000, with astronauts from NASA, Roscosmos, ESA, JAXA, and CSA working together in microgravity. The ISS serves as a laboratory for scientific research that could not be conducted on Earth. Source: NASA.gov
facts.statement: The International Space Station has been continuously inhabited since November 2, 2000.
facts.wowFactor: The ISS is so large you can see it from Earth with the naked eye — it appears as a bright star moving across the night sky, moving faster than a shooting star.
facts.visualDescription: The International Space Station in orbit with its solar arrays extended, Earth visible below with city lights at night

variants.question: Which orbiting laboratory has been continuously inhabited by astronauts since 2000?
What is the name of the international space station built by five space agencies?
True or false: The International Space Station has been continuously inhabited since 2000.
The ___ has been the largest structure in space and continuously inhabited since November 2, 2000.

facts.quizQuestion: Which space this mission was built through an unprecedented collaboration between NASA, Roscosmos, ESA, JAXA, and CSA — five different space agencies?
facts.explanation: The International Space Station was built through collaboration between five major space agencies: NASA (USA), Roscosmos (Russia), the European Space Agency (ESA), JAXA (Japan), and the Canadian Space Agency (CSA). This unprecedented international cooperation resulted in the largest structure ever built in space. Source: NASA.gov
facts.statement: Five space agencies collaborated to build the International Space Station: NASA, Roscosmos, ESA, JAXA, and CSA.
facts.wowFactor: The ISS represents Cold War rivals USA and Russia working together peacefully — American and Russian astronauts and cosmonauts live and work together in orbit, proving that science transcends geopolitics.
facts.visualDescription: Flags of five nations displayed at the International Space Station control center with astronauts working together

variants.question: Which space station was operated by five space agencies — NASA, Roscosmos, ESA, JAXA, and CSA?
What space station was built by collaboration of five nations' space agencies?
True or false: The International Space Station was built by five space agencies.
The ___ was built through collaboration between NASA, Roscosmos, ESA, JAXA, and CSA.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the name of NASA's program to return humans to the Moon and eventually send them to Mars?
facts.explanation: Artemis is NASA's ambitious program to return humans to the Moon using modern technology, including new spacecraft (Orion), launch systems (Space Launch System), and lunar landers. Named after Apollo's twin sister in Greek mythology, the program aims to establish a sustainable presence on the Moon and eventually send humans to Mars. Source: NASA.gov
facts.statement: Artemis is NASA's program to return humans to the Moon and eventually send them to Mars.
facts.wowFactor: The Artemis program aims to land the first woman and first person of color on the Moon — a historic milestone for space exploration.
facts.visualDescription: Orion spacecraft approaching the Moon with the Space Launch System rocket visible in the background, depicting future Artemis missions

variants.question: Which NASA program aims to return humans to the Moon?
What is the modern successor to Apollo that will return humans to the Moon?
True or false: Artemis is NASA's program to return humans to the Moon.
___ is NASA's modern program to return humans to the Moon and eventually send them to Mars.

facts.quizQuestion: Which NASA mission in November 2022 was the first uncrewed test flight of the Space Launch System rocket and Orion spacecraft around the Moon?
facts.explanation: Artemis I was an uncrewed test flight of NASA's new Space Launch System (SLS) rocket and Orion spacecraft. Launched on November 16, 2022, it orbited the Moon for 25.5 days and returned to Earth, successfully validating the systems needed for future crewed Artemis missions to the Moon. Source: NASA.gov
facts.statement: Artemis I was the first uncrewed test flight of the SLS rocket and Orion spacecraft, launched in November 2022.
facts.wowFactor: Artemis I's Orion capsule traveled 270,000 miles from Earth to the Moon and back, farther than any spacecraft with a human-rated design had ever traveled since Apollo.
facts.visualDescription: Space Launch System rocket lifting off with Orion capsule at the top, showing the most powerful rocket since Saturn V

variants.question: Which Artemis mission was the first test flight of the Space Launch System and Orion?
Which NASA mission in November 2022 tested the Space Launch System and Orion spacecraft by orbiting the Moon?
True or false: Artemis I was an uncrewed test flight around the Moon.
___ was the uncrewed test flight of the Space Launch System and Orion that orbited the Moon in November 2022.

facts.quizQuestion: Which spacecraft is the most distant human-made object from Earth?
facts.explanation: Voyager 1, launched in 1977, is over 15 billion miles from Earth and entered interstellar space in 2012. Source: NASA.gov
facts.statement: Voyager 1 is the most distant human-made object from Earth.
facts.wowFactor: Voyager 1 is so far away that signals traveling at the speed of light take over 22 hours to reach it — and it's still sending data back.
facts.visualDescription: Small golden spacecraft with large dish antenna sailing through dark interstellar void, a faint dot of sunlight far behind

variants.question: Voyager 1 holds which record among spacecraft?
Which 1977 spacecraft entered interstellar space in 2012 and is now over 15 billion miles from Earth?
True or false: Voyager 1 is the most distant human-made object from Earth.
The most distant human-made object from Earth is ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Which NASA this mission carries a gold-plated record with greetings in 55 languages and sounds from Earth?
facts.explanation: Both Voyager 1 and Voyager 2 carry identical gold-plated records with music, greetings, and natural sounds from Earth, intended as messages to any extraterrestrial life. Source: NASA.gov
facts.statement: The Voyager program spacecraft carry golden records intended as messages to extraterrestrial life.
facts.wowFactor: The golden records include a greeting from UN Secretary-General Kurt Waldheim, Chuck Berry's 'Johnny B. Goode', and the sound of a human heartbeat — a message in a bottle to the cosmos.
facts.visualDescription: Golden record floating in space next to Voyager spacecraft, with musical notes and geometric patterns etched on its surface, Earth visible in the distance

variants.question: The golden record is part of which NASA program?
Which spacecraft launched in 1977 carries a gold-plated record with messages and music from Earth for potential extraterrestrial civilizations?
True or false: The Voyager spacecraft carry golden records with cultural content from Earth.
The ___ program sent golden records carrying Earth's sounds and greetings into deep space.

facts.quizQuestion: Which spacecraft is the only one to have visited all four gas giants: Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune?
facts.explanation: Voyager 2, launched in 1977, is the only spacecraft to visit all four gas giants. It flew past Jupiter (1979), Saturn (1981), Uranus (1986), and Neptune (1989). Source: NASA.gov
facts.statement: Voyager 2 is the only spacecraft to have visited all four gas giants.
facts.wowFactor: Voyager 2's Grand Tour of the outer planets required a rare planetary alignment that only occurs once every 176 years — it took advantage of gravitational assists to reach all four giants in a single mission.
facts.visualDescription: Voyager spacecraft approaching four gas giants in sequence, from warm colors of Jupiter to blue of Neptune, showing the scale of the outer solar system

variants.question: Which achievement is unique to Voyager 2?
Which Voyager spacecraft took advantage of a rare planetary alignment to visit Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune?
True or false: Voyager 1 visited all four gas giants.
___ is the only spacecraft to have visited all four gas giants.

facts.quizQuestion: Which spacecraft was the first human-made object to enter interstellar space?

Webb, JWST observes primarily in the infrared spectrum and is about 100 times more powerful than Hubble. It orbits the Sun at Lagrange Point 2. Source: NASA.gov
facts.statement: The James Webb Space Telescope was launched on Christmas Day 2021 and is the most powerful space telescope ever built.
facts.wowFactor: JWST's primary mirror is made of beryllium coated with gold (to reflect infrared), it cost ~$10 billion, and it can see galaxies formed just 100 million years after the Big Bang — peering nearly to the beginning of the universe.
facts.visualDescription: Large golden hexagonal mirror segments arranged in honeycomb pattern with five-layer sunshield below, floating in space with stars visible beyond, deployed configuration in orbit around the Sun

facts.quizQuestion: Which space this optical instrument orbits the Sun at Lagrange point 2, about 1 million miles from Earth — unlike Hubble which orbits Earth directly?
facts.explanation: The James Webb Space Telescope orbits the Sun at Lagrange Point 2 (L2), a gravitational equilibrium point where the Earth, Sun, and telescope maintain a stable configuration with minimal fuel consumption. L2 is about 930,000 miles (1.5 million kilometers) from Earth on the opposite side from the Sun. Source: NASA.gov
facts.statement: The James Webb Space Telescope orbits the Sun at Lagrange Point 2, far from Earth.
facts.wowFactor: JWST's position at L2 means it maintains a stable orbit that 'falls' exactly as fast as it orbits, never drifting closer or farther away — it's in a perfect gravitational balance point where it can stay operational for decades without significant fuel consumption.
facts.visualDescription: Diagram showing Earth, Sun, and JWST's position at Lagrange Point 2 with arrows indicating gravitational forces in equilibrium, not orbiting Earth but in a stable configuration far beyond the Moon

facts.quizQuestion: Which space observatory discovered over 2,600 confirmed exoplanets, more than any other observatory?
facts.explanation: The Kepler Space Telescope, launched in 2009, discovered 2,662 confirmed exoplanets by the time its mission ended in 2018. This represents more than half of all exoplanet discoveries. Kepler used the transit method, detecting the tiny brightness dips as planets crossed in front of their host stars. Source: NASA.gov
facts.statement: The Kepler Space Telescope discovered over 2,600 exoplanets, more than any other observatory.
facts.wowFactor: Kepler discovered more exoplanets than all other methods combined — each planet detected by a brightness dip so tiny (1/10,000th to 1/100th) that on a clear night on Earth, it would be like detecting a car's headlights on the Moon.

facts.quizQuestion: Which telescope found exoplanets by detecting tiny dips in starlight when planets cross in front of their host stars?
facts.explanation: The Kepler Space Telescope used the transit photometry method to discover exoplanets. When a planet passes in front of its star (transits), it blocks a tiny amount of light, causing a characteristic dip in the star's brightness. By monitoring these dips for thousands of stars, Kepler could detect planets and even determine their size. Source: NASA.gov
facts.statement: The Kepler Space Telescope used the transit method to find exoplanets by detecting dips in starlight.
facts.wowFactor: Kepler's sensitivity was so precise it could detect a brightness dip of just 0.01% — equivalent to a firefly passing in front of a distant car's headlights, yet this tiny signal revealed 2,600+ entire worlds.
facts.visualDescription: Diagram showing a star with a planet crossing in front, with graph showing periodic dips in star brightness corresponding to planetary transits, exoplanet's orbit highlighted

facts.explanation: JWST launched on December 25, 2021, aboard an Ariane 5 rocket from French Guiana. After decades of delays and cost overruns, it arrived at its L2 orbit and began delivering stunning images in 2022. Source: NASA.gov
facts.statement: The James Webb Space Telescope launched on Christmas Day 2021.
facts.wowFactor: JWST was originally scheduled to launch in 2007 and cost $1 billion — it finally launched 14 years late at 10x the original budget, but the results have been worth every penny.
facts.visualDescription: Golden hexagonal mirror telescope unfolding its sunshield in space, stars visible behind it, Christmas theme subtle
